details.reason: golden has no quoted_span — run draft_quote_spans.py first

samples.claim: As of 2024, Spotify reported 425 million free (ad-supported) users and 263 million premium subscribers across 184 markets (Spotify).

The service offers basic features free with advertisements and limited control, while additional features like offline listening and commercial-free playback are offered via paid subscriptions (Spotif
Since Spotify pays approximately **70% of its total revenue** to rights holders (Criticism of Spotify), the low ARPU from free users drags down the entire payout pool.
In 2024, Spotify generated €15.7 billion in revenue (Spotify).
However, with over 100 million tracks and trillions of annual streams (Spotify), the per-stream value becomes vanishingly small.
Spotify faces intense competition from Apple Music, Amazon Music, YouTube Music, and others (Music streaming service).
The **price elasticity** of the streaming market means significant price increases would drive users to competitors or back to piracy (Music piracy).
Spotify's pricing power is constrained by the **product bundling** economics of the streaming market (Product bundling).

Spotify must cover its own costs before distributing royalties: content delivery network costs, bandwidth, server infrastructure, employee salaries (7,261 employees as of December 2024), marketing, an
Spotify uses a **pro-rata (market share) distribution model**, not a user-centric one (Criticism of Spotify).
Spotify has resisted this model, citing technical complexity, though Deezer has experimented with it (Deezer).
It pays **rights holders** — typically record labels, music publishers, and collecting societies (Royalty payment).
- Paid to **publishers and performing rights organizations (PROs)** like ASCAP, BMI, SESAC (Performance rights organisation)
Every stream involves **two distinct copyrights** (Copyright):
The **mechanical royalty** covers the reproduction of the composition (Music royalties).
These labels have **recording contracts** that typically give artists a **15-25% royalty rate** on revenue after deductions (Recording contract).
**Independent record labels** (Independent record label) typically offer higher royalty splits (50/50 or better) but lack the marketing muscle and playlist placement power of majors.
The **independent music** sector has grown significantly through digital distribution (Independent music).
In 2023, they discontinued physical distribution and shifted focus to digital-only (CD Baby).
In the **album era** (mid-1960s through mid-2000s), recorded music was the primary revenue source (Album era).

tier_b_items.reason: Identifies ARPU disparity and pro-rata as structural patterns.
Explains why pro-rata and ARPU cause low per-stream payouts.
No explicit cross-referencing of different source types.
Pro-rata vs user-centric synthesis yields novel causal insight.
No acknowledgment of limitations or counterarguments to claims.
Report ends mid-sentence; no actionable conclusion section.

verdicts: Does the report explicitly answer 'why ~$0.003 per stream'?
Are exactly 4 causal layers defined (revenue / royalty / artist / systemic)?
Does L1 cite >= 3 wiki articles on streaming revenue / ad+sub model?
Does L2 cite >= 3 wiki articles on royalty pool / pro-rata vs user-centric?
Does L3 cite >= 5 reddit threads from indie musicians on take-home math?
Does L4 cite >= 5 reddit threads on touring vs streaming economics?
Is a multi-layer causal hierarchy presented in markdown (indented bullets)?
Is a 'pay-per-stream comparison' table presented for >= 5 platforms (Spotify/Apple/Tidal/YT Music/Bandcamp)?
Are per-stream payouts cited per platform with reddit or wiki source?
Are >= 3 alternative revenue paths (Bandcamp / Patreon / direct-to-fan) enumerated?
Are >= 10 shopping books on music industry cited with price + rating?
Are >= 30 reddit threads from at least 4 music-industry sub-forums cited?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles cited?
Does the report cite the major-label cut (typically ~50-60% of royalty)?
Does the report quantify indie distributor (DistroKid/TuneCore) cut explicitly?
Are at least 3 cross-source divergences surfaced (label-PR claim vs artist-reddit reality)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Is the report 3500-8000 words / >= 25 paragraphs and starts with L1?
